passages.explanation: Matthew opens with a genealogy — the book of the generation of Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham. He traces the line through Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Judah, and onward through David and the kings of Judah, through the exile, and down to Joseph, the husband of Mary, from whom Jesus was born. Matthew structures the genealogy into three sets of fourteen generations: Abraham to David, David to the exile, the exile to Christ. The genealogy is deliberately shaped to make a theological point — Jesus is the rightful heir to David's throne and the fulfilment of God's promise to Abraham. Notable inclusions: Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, and Bathsheba — four women with irregular or Gentile backgrounds, signalling from the very first page that God's plan has always included outsiders.

The birth of Jesus happens like this: Mary is pledged to be married to Joseph, but before they come together, she's found to be pregnant through the Holy Spirit. Joseph, being a righteous man and not wanting to disgrace her publicly, plans to divorce her quietly. But an angel of the Lord appears to him in a dream: Joseph, son of David, don't be afraid to take Mary as your wife — what's conceived in her is from the Holy Spirit. She will give birth to a son, and you are to name him Jesus, because he will save his people from their sins. Joseph's righteousness shows in his restraint — he could have made an example of her, but he chooses mercy even before he understands the full picture.

Matthew adds his first of many Old Testament fulfilment formulas: all this took place to fulfil what the Lord had spoken through the prophet — the virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and they will call him Immanuel, meaning God with us. He's quoting Isaiah 7:14. Joseph wakes up, does what the angel commanded, takes Mary home as his wife, but doesn't have sexual relations with her until she gives birth to a son. He names him Jesus. Matthew's entire gospel is built around this pattern — showing that Jesus doesn't just happen to resemble Old Testament hopes, he deliberately fulfils them. The name Immanuel sets the tone for the whole book: God is no longer distant. He's moved in.
